questions.question_text:
The British East India Company's administration implemented several laws that directly challenged deeply ingrained social customs prevalent in India. Sati was outlawed in 1829, widow remarriage was legalized in 1856, and efforts were made to suppress female infanticide. These interventions, though intended to be progressive, were perceived by many Indians as a direct assault on their traditions and religious beliefs, fostering resentment and contributing to the simmering discontent.

(d) They improved the relationship between the British and the Indian aristocracy.

  • This led to increased communal tensions and mistrust between the two communities, fostering disunity.

questions.question_text: The First War of Independence led to heightened racial antagonism. What evidence from its aftermath demonstrates this consequence?

• The British developed a deep distrust of Indians, leading to discriminatory practices.
  • There was increased segregation in social spaces and institutions.
  • The British adopted a policy of deliberately creating suspicion and mistrust between different communities to prevent them from uniting against British rule.

questions.section_ref: CONSEQUENCES OF THE FIRST WAR OF INDEPENDENCE > Changes in Administration > Racial Antagonism

Read the text and state one impact of the 1857 revolt on the British policy regarding religious interference in India. 

"After the revolt, the British government adopted a policy of non-interference in the religious and social affairs of the people. They realized that their previous attempts to reform Indian society had been a major cause of the revolt."
questions.model_answer_text: • The British adopted a policy of non-interference in the religious and social affairs of the Indian people.
questions.section_ref: CONSEQUENCES OF THE FIRST WAR OF INDEPENDENCE > Changes in Administration > Religious Changes

questions.question_text: Read the excerpt and identify two ways economic exploitation worsened after 1857: "Following the 1857 revolt, the British intensified their efforts to control India's resources and markets. British industries further exploited India's raw materials, while finished goods from Britain flooded Indian markets. This led to a decline in local industries and increased dependence on British products."
questions.model_answer_text:
• Increased extraction of raw materials from India to benefit British industries.
  • Flooding of Indian markets with British manufactured goods, damaging local industries.
questions.section_ref: CONSEQUENCES OF THE FIRST WAR OF INDEPENDENCE > Changes in Administration > Economic Exploitation

• It created widespread resentment among Indian rulers who viewed it as unjust.
  • It instilled fear and distrust, as other states worried about similar annexations.
  • It fueled anti-British sentiment and contributed to the growing unrest.

questions.question_text: Read the following. How did the British disrespect shown to Bahadur Shah contribute to the 1857 revolt? Explain two points.
questions.model_answer_text:
• Humiliating treatment: The British humiliated Bahadur Shah by ordering him to leave the Red Fort.
  • Ending the Mughal Dynasty: The British declared that Bahadur Shah's successors would not be recognized as emperors, ending the Mughal dynasty.
questions.section_ref: Political Causes > Disrespect Shown to Bahadur Shah

questions.question_text: Nana Saheb was the adopted son of Peshwa Baji Rao II. After Peshwa's death, the British stopped Nana Saheb's pension and also ordered him to leave the family residence in Poona. Why did this treatment towards Nana Saheb cause resentment against the British?
questions.model_answer_text:
• It was seen as a violation of the Doctrine of Adoption, affecting not only Nana Saheb but also other potential heirs.
  • It was viewed as a personal insult and injustice, as Nana Saheb was denied his rightful inheritance and status.
  • It highlighted the British disregard for Indian customs and traditions, fueling anti-British sentiment.

questions.question_text: Read the text: "The British government was an absentee sovereign. The real seat of power was located in England." What effect did this 'absentee sovereignty' have on the Indian population?

questions.question_text: The British banned sati and encouraged widow remarriage. How did Indians interpret these actions and why did they resent them?

• Many Indians viewed these reforms as a direct interference with their established social and religious customs.
  • They believed the British were attempting to Christianize and Westernize Indian society by undermining traditional practices.
  • The speed and forceful nature of these interventions, without considering local sensitivities, caused resentment.

questions.question_text: Read the statement: "Introduction of railways and telegraphs were viewed with suspicion." List two reasons for this apprehension in 1857.

• People feared that the railways and telegraphs were intended to break down the caste system by enabling all castes to mix freely.

questions.question_text: Read the excerpt: "Indians were treated with contempt and were called names like 'nigger' and 'suar'. They were looked down upon". How did this racial discrimination contribute to the 1857 revolt?
questions.model_answer_text:
• Racial discrimination created deep resentment among Indians.
  • It fostered a feeling of humiliation and inferiority.
  • It led to a loss of respect for the British and their authority.
  • It fueled the desire to overthrow British rule and restore Indian dignity.
questions.section_ref: Socio-Religious Causes > Policy of Racial Discrimination

questions.question_text: Analyze how corruption in the British administration, as perceived by the Indian populace, contributed to the First War of Independence.
questions.model_answer_text:
• Corruption fostered resentment: Rampant corruption among British officials created widespread discontent among the Indian population.
  • Inefficient justice: A corrupt judicial system denied Indians fair trials and justice, leading to anger.
  • Bribery and extortion: Bribery and extortion by officials impoverished many, exacerbating economic hardships.
  • Lack of accountability: Absence of proper oversight allowed corruption to flourish, undermining trust in British rule.
questions.section_ref: Socio-Religious Causes > Corruption in Administration

questions.question_text: Read the excerpt: "Land revenue was increased substantially. The poor became poorer, and the peasants were increasingly dispossessed of their land." Identify *two* ways oppression of the poor contributed to the 1857 revolt.
questions.model_answer_text:
• Increased land revenue led to widespread discontent among peasants, who formed a significant part of the population. This resulted in direct participation in the revolt.
  • Dispossession of land created a class of landless laborers who felt resentment towards the British, fueling their desire to fight for their rights and livelihoods.
questions.section_ref: Socio-Religious Causes > Oppression of the Poor

questions.question_text: Read the statement: "Missionaries openly criticized Hindu and Islamic beliefs, leading to widespread resentment." How did this activity contribute to the 1857 revolt?
questions.model_answer_text:
• The open criticism of Hindu and Islamic beliefs by missionaries was perceived as an attack on the traditional religions of India.
  • This created fear and resentment among the Indian populace, who felt their culture and religion were under threat.
  • The activities fueled suspicions that the British aimed to convert Indians to Christianity, further alienating them from British rule.
questions.section_ref: Socio-Religious Causes > Activities of Missionaries

questions.question_text: A contemporary observer wrote: "...the introduction of Western education is undermining our traditional values and beliefs..." How did such sentiments contribute to the 1857 revolt?
questions.model_answer_text:
• Many Indians feared that Western education was a tool for Christian conversion and cultural domination.
  • They believed it threatened their traditional social and religious structures, causing resentment.
  • Western education was seen as undermining the authority of traditional religious leaders, who then opposed British rule.
questions.section_ref: Socio-Religious Causes > Fears Regarding Western Education

questions.question_text:
The British policy of expansion created resentment amongst Indian rulers and the general population. Discuss the following:

(i.) How was the Policy of Subsidiary Alliance a cause of resentment? [3]

(ii.) State two examples of annexation on the pretext of alleged misrule. [3]

(iii.) In what ways was the Doctrine of Lapse implemented and why did it cause widespread discontent? [4]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • The Subsidiary Alliance system forced Indian rulers to disband their own armies and maintain British troops in their territory at their own expense.
  • This drained their resources and made them dependent on the British.
  • The rulers lost their autonomy and felt humiliated by British interference in their affairs, thus causing resentment.

(ii.)

  • Awadh in 1856: Annexed by Lord Dalhousie citing alleged misgovernment and maladministration.
  • Hyderabad: Forced to cede territories under the Subsidiary Alliance, citing administrative failures and unpaid dues.

(iii.)

  • The Doctrine of Lapse was implemented by annexing states where the ruler died without a natural male heir.
  • Satara (1848), Nagpur (1853), and Jhansi (1854) were annexed under this policy.
  • It caused discontent because it violated the Indian tradition of adopted heirs and was seen as a blatant grab for territory by the British, thus undermining the authority and legitimacy of Indian rulers.
  • This created a sense of insecurity among other rulers who feared a similar fate, leading to widespread discontent.
questions.section_ref: Political Causes > Policy of Expansion > By Using the Doctrine of Lapse

questions.question_text:
The British actions deeply offended Indian sensibilities and triggered widespread anger. With reference to this context, answer the following:

(i.) How did the British disrespect shown to Bahadur Shah contribute to the revolt? [3]

(ii.) What was the reason for the British to end the pension of Nana Saheb? [3]

(iii.) Explain the concept of "Absentee Sovereignty" and how it fuelled resentment. [4]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • Lord Dalhousie ordered that Bahadur Shah's successors would not be recognized as emperors and would only be called princes.
  • Lord Canning announced that Bahadur Shah would be the last Mughal King and his successors would have to leave the Red Fort.
  • These actions deeply humiliated the Mughal dynasty, which had ruled India for centuries, and angered many Indians who still revered Bahadur Shah as their symbolic leader, thus triggering widespread anger and resentment.

(ii.)

  • Nana Saheb was the adopted son of Peshwa Baji Rao II.
  • The British refused to recognize him as the heir to the Peshwa and stopped his pension after Baji Rao II's death.
  • This act was seen as unjust and disrespectful to Nana Saheb and his lineage, contributing to his resentment against the British and motivating him to participate actively in the revolt.

(iii.)

  • "Absentee Sovereignty" refers to the fact that the British rulers were physically absent from India, governing from afar.
  • This resulted in a lack of understanding of local conditions and a disconnect between the rulers and the ruled.
  • Indians felt alienated and neglected by the British government, which was perceived as being more interested in exploiting the country's resources than in serving its people.
  • This feeling of alienation and neglect, combined with the perceived exploitation of India, fueled widespread resentment against the British.
questions.section_ref: Political Causes > Absentee Sovereignty of the British

questions.question_text:
The Policy of Expansion was a key political cause of the 1857 revolt. Discuss the following aspects:

(i.) Explain how the British used "outright wars" to expand their control in India. [3]

(ii.) State three terms of the Subsidiary Alliance. [3]

(iii.) How did the annexation of Awadh on the pretext of alleged misrule contribute to the uprising of 1857? [4]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • The British engaged in direct military conflicts to annex territories.
  • Examples include the Anglo-Mysore Wars, which resulted in the annexation of Mysore territories.
  • The Anglo-Sikh Wars led to the annexation of Punjab. These wars demonstrated the British desire for territorial expansion through military force.

(ii.)

  • An Indian ruler entering into the Subsidiary Alliance had to keep British troops in his territory.
  • The ruler had to pay for the maintenance of these troops.
  • The ruler could not enter into agreements with any other power without the permission of the British.

(iii.)

  • Awadh was annexed in 1856 by Lord Dalhousie on the grounds of alleged misgovernment.
  • This action was deeply unpopular as it was perceived as unjust and arbitrary.
  • The annexation resulted in the disbanding of the Awadh army, leaving many soldiers unemployed and resentful.
  • It also hurt the aristocracy and zamindars in Awadh. The annexation caused widespread anger and resentment, thus contributing to the revolt.
questions.section_ref: Political Causes > Policy of Expansion > On the Pretext of Alleged Misrule

questions.question_text:
The British employed various expansionist policies that significantly impacted India.

(i.) What were the major wars fought by the British as part of their policy of expansion? [3]

(ii.) How did the Subsidiary Alliance system contribute to British expansion? [3]

(iii.) Explain the main provisions of the Doctrine of Lapse and its impact on Indian states. [4]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • The Anglo-Mysore Wars: Series of conflicts against the Kingdom of Mysore.
  • The Anglo-Maratha Wars: Resulted in the annexation of Maratha territories.
  • The Anglo-Sikh Wars: Led to the annexation of Punjab.

(ii.)

  • Indian rulers were forced to keep British troops in their territory.
  • Rulers had to pay for the maintenance of these troops.
  • Indian rulers lost their independence and control over their foreign affairs.

(iii.)

  • The Doctrine stated that if an Indian ruler died without a natural heir, their territory would be annexed by the British.
  • It led to the annexation of several states, including Satara, Jhansi, and Nagpur.
  • Indian rulers felt insecure and resentful towards the British due to this policy.
  • It created widespread resentment and contributed to the growing discontent that fueled the 1857 revolt.
questions.section_ref: The First War of Independence > CAUSES OF THE FIRST WAR OF INDEPENDENCE > Political Causes > Policy of Expansion > By Using the Doctrine of Lapse

questions.question_text:
British expansionist policies were a major source of discontent.

(i.) Briefly describe the concept of 'outright wars' employed for British expansion. [3]

(ii.) State any three provisions of the Subsidiary Alliance. [3]

(iii.) How did the annexation of Awadh on the pretext of alleged misrule contribute to the 1857 revolt? [4]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • Outright wars involved direct military conquest and annexation of territories.
  • The British East India Company engaged in several wars to expand its control.
  • These wars included conflicts against Mysore, the Marathas, and the Sikhs.

(ii.)

  • Indian rulers were not allowed to have independent armed forces.
  • They were to be protected by the Company's troops, which they would have to pay for.
  • They could not enter into alliances with other powers without the Company's permission.

(iii.)

  • Awadh was annexed in 1856 on the grounds of alleged misgovernance.
  • The annexation was widely perceived as unjust and high-handed.
  • It angered the local population, including sepoys who came from Awadh.
  • The annexation also dispossessed many landlords and nobles, adding to the resentment.
questions.section_ref: The First War of Independence > CAUSES OF THE FIRST WAR OF INDEPENDENCE > Political Causes > Policy of Expansion > On the Pretext of Alleged Misrule

questions.question_text:
The British policy of expansion led to widespread resentment among Indian rulers and the general population.

(i.) Explain how the policy of Subsidiary Alliance was implemented and its effects. [4]

(ii.) Name any three states annexed by the British using the Doctrine of Lapse. [3]

(iii.) What was the 'pretext of alleged misrule,' and which state was annexed under this pretext? [3]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • Indian rulers were forced to accept British troops in their territory.
  • They had to pay for the maintenance of these troops.
  • Rulers lost the right to maintain their own independent armies.
  • They also lost control over their foreign affairs, as they could not enter into alliances without British permission.

(ii.)

  • Satara
  • Jhansi
  • Nagpur

(iii.)

  • 'Pretext of alleged misrule' was a justification used by the British to annex states on the grounds that they were not being properly governed.
  • Awadh was annexed under this pretext in 1856.
  • This caused great resentment among the people of Awadh and contributed to the uprising.
questions.section_ref: The First War of Independence > CAUSES OF THE FIRST WAR OF INDEPENDENCE > Political Causes > Policy of Expansion > On the Pretext of Alleged Misrule

questions.question_text:
British expansionist policies greatly impacted Indian rulers and territories.

(i.) Explain how outright wars contributed to the expansion of British power in India. [4]

(ii.) State two negative consequences of the Subsidiary Alliance for Indian rulers. [3]

(iii.) How did the Doctrine of Lapse create resentment among the Indian rulers? [3]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • The British East India Company engaged in direct military conflicts with Indian states.
  • Victories in these wars allowed them to annex territories directly.
  • Examples include the Anglo-Mysore Wars, Anglo-Maratha Wars, and Anglo-Sikh Wars.
  • These wars led to the consolidation of British power and control over vast areas of India.

(ii.)

  • Indian rulers lost their independence and autonomy.
  • They were forced to maintain British troops at their own expense, draining their resources.

(iii.)

  • The Doctrine of Lapse allowed the British to annex states if the ruler died without a natural male heir.
  • This created a sense of insecurity among rulers who feared losing their kingdoms.
  • It was perceived as an unjust and arbitrary policy, leading to widespread resentment.
questions.section_ref: The First War of Independence > CAUSES OF THE FIRST WAR OF INDEPENDENCE > Political Causes > Policy of Expansion > By Using the Doctrine of Lapse

questions.question_text:
The British East India Company used various policies to expand its control over India.

(i.) What was the main objective of the Subsidiary Alliance? [3]

(ii.) Explain the main features of the Doctrine of Lapse. [4]

(iii.) How did the annexation of Indian territories under the pretext of alleged misrule lead to widespread discontent? [3]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • The main objective was to bring Indian states under British control without direct military intervention.
  • It aimed to reduce the power of Indian rulers.
  • It was also meant to prevent them from forming alliances against the British.

(ii.)

  • The Doctrine of Lapse allowed the British to annex any Indian state where the ruler died without a natural male heir.
  • Adoptions were not recognized as legal heirs.
  • This policy was used to annex several states like Satara, Jhansi, and Nagpur.
  • It led to great resentment among Indian rulers.

(iii.)

  • The annexation of territories like Awadh on the grounds of misrule was seen as unjust.
  • It angered the local population and the sepoys, many of whom came from these regions.
  • The annexation resulted in the loss of power and status for many local elites, fueling discontent.
questions.section_ref: The First War of Independence > CAUSES OF THE FIRST WAR OF INDEPENDENCE > Political Causes > Policy of Expansion > On the Pretext of Alleged Misrule

questions.question_text:
The expansionist policies of the British East India Company were a major source of conflict.

(i.) Describe the role of 'outright wars' in British expansionist policy. [3]

(ii.) What were the conditions imposed on Indian rulers under the Subsidiary Alliance system? Give any three points. [3]

(iii.) Discuss the annexation of Awadh and its impact on the revolt of 1857. [4]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • Outright wars involved direct military conquest of Indian territories.
  • The British engaged in several wars to achieve their expansionist goals.
  • These wars often resulted in the annexation of defeated states, increasing British control.

(ii.)

  • Indian rulers had to accept British troops in their territory.
  • The rulers had to pay for the maintenance of these troops.
  • They could not enter into alliances with other powers without British approval.

(iii.)

  • Awadh was annexed in 1856 on the pretext of misgovernance.
  • This annexation was perceived as unjust and angered the local population.
  • Many sepoys came from Awadh and felt their families had been wronged.
  • The annexation fueled the resentment and contributed significantly to the outbreak of the 1857 revolt.
questions.section_ref: The First War of Independence > CAUSES OF THE FIRST WAR OF INDEPENDENCE > Political Causes > Policy of Expansion > On the Pretext of Alleged Misrule

questions.question_text:
The British employed a range of policies to expand their influence in India.

(i.) Explain the main features of the Subsidiary Alliance system. [3]

(ii.) How did the Doctrine of Lapse contribute to the expansion of British territories in India? [4]

(iii.) What was the justification used by the British for annexing Awadh, and why did this create resentment? [3]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • Indian rulers were forced to keep British troops stationed in their territory.
  • The rulers had to pay for the maintenance of these troops.
  • The rulers were not allowed to maintain their own independent armed forces.

(ii.)

  • The Doctrine of Lapse allowed the British to annex Indian states if the ruler died without a natural male heir.
  • This policy disregarded the Indian custom of adoption.
  • Several states, including Satara, Jhansi and Nagpur, were annexed using this doctrine.
  • This policy created a sense of insecurity and resentment among Indian rulers.

(iii.)

  • The British annexed Awadh on the pretext of 'alleged misrule' or inefficient governance.
  • This was seen as unjust and arbitrary, as the administration in Awadh was not necessarily worse than in other states.
  • The annexation caused widespread resentment because it dispossessed many local elites and angered the sepoys who came from the region.
questions.section_ref: The First War of Independence > CAUSES OF THE FIRST WAR OF INDEPENDENCE > Political Causes > Policy of Expansion > On the Pretext of Alleged Misrule

questions.question_text:
British expansionist policies had profound consequences on the political landscape of India.

(i.) Describe the 'outright wars' undertaken by the British to expand their territory. [3]

(ii.) Explain how the Subsidiary Alliance weakened the Indian rulers. [4]

(iii.) Explain the Doctrine of Lapse and name two states that were annexed under this policy. [3]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • Outright wars involved direct military conquest by the British East India Company.
  • These wars resulted in the annexation of territories.
  • Major wars included the Anglo-Mysore Wars, Anglo-Maratha Wars, and Anglo-Sikh Wars.

(ii.)

  • Indian rulers were forced to accept British troops in their territory.
  • They had to pay for the maintenance of these troops, draining their resources.
  • They lost the right to have independent armies.
  • They could not enter into alliances with other powers without British consent.

(iii.)

  • The Doctrine of Lapse allowed the British to annex states if the ruler died without a natural male heir.
  • Satara and Jhansi were annexed under this policy.
  • This policy created resentment and insecurity among Indian rulers.
questions.section_ref: The First War of Independence > CAUSES OF THE FIRST WAR OF INDEPENDENCE > Political Causes > Policy of Expansion > By Using the Doctrine of Lapse

questions.question_text:
The British Policy of Expansion had a significant impact on the Indian subcontinent.

(i.) How did the British utilize 'outright wars' to expand their territory in India? [3]

(ii.) Explain the term 'Subsidiary Alliance' and its effect on the Indian states that accepted it. [4]

(iii.) How did the British use the pretext of 'alleged misrule' to annex Indian territories? Give one example. [3]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • The British East India Company engaged in direct military conflicts to conquer and annex territories.
  • They waged wars against various Indian powers, such as Mysore, the Marathas and the Sikhs.
  • Victories in these wars allowed them to expand their dominion and establish control over new regions.

(ii.)

  • The Subsidiary Alliance was a system where Indian rulers were forced to accept British troops in their territory.
  • These rulers had to pay for the maintenance of these troops.
  • They were also prevented from entering into alliances with other powers without British consent.
  • This system effectively reduced the independence of Indian rulers and brought them under British control.

(iii.)

  • The British used the pretext of 'alleged misrule' to justify the annexation of Indian territories by claiming that the rulers were incompetent or corrupt.
  • They argued that they were acting in the best interests of the people by taking over administration.
  • Awadh was annexed in 1856 on the grounds of alleged misrule, causing widespread resentment.
questions.section_ref: The First War of Independence > CAUSES OF THE FIRST WAR OF INDEPENDENCE > Political Causes > Policy of Expansion > On the Pretext of Alleged Misrule

questions.question_text:
The British policies significantly impacted the socio-religious life of Indians, causing widespread resentment and contributing to the 1857 uprising.

(i.) How did the British interfere with social customs in India, and what was the impact? [3]

(ii.) Explain how activities of missionaries contributed to the growing unrest among the Indian population. [3]

(iii.) What were the apprehensions regarding modern innovations introduced by the British? [4]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • The British interfered with social customs by abolishing practices like Sati and encouraging widow remarriage.
  • These reforms, though intended to be progressive, were seen as an intrusion into the traditional social fabric.
  • This interference caused resentment among orthodox sections of society who felt their religious and social values were being undermined.

(ii.)

  • Christian missionaries were allowed to propagate their religion and convert people.
  • Their activities, including opening schools and hospitals, were viewed with suspicion.
  • Many Indians feared that these activities were aimed at undermining their own religions and converting them to Christianity.
  • This led to a fear of cultural and religious subjugation, contributing to the overall unrest.

(iii.)

  • Modern innovations like railways and telegraph were viewed with suspicion.
  • Many believed that these innovations were a conspiracy to break down the caste system.
  • The introduction of Western education was also met with resistance as it challenged traditional learning and values.
  • People feared that these innovations would lead to the erosion of their traditional way of life and forced assimilation into Western culture.
questions.section_ref: Socio-Religious Causes

questions.question_text:
Racial discrimination and policies impacting religious places stirred resentment among the Indian populace, contributing significantly to the 1857 revolt.

(i.) Explain how the policy of racial discrimination by the British fueled resentment among the Indian population. [3]

(ii.) How did the British policy of taxing religious places affect sentiments in India? [4]

(iii.) In what ways did the 'Law of Property' contribute to the grievances of the Indian people? [3]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • The British showed racial superiority towards Indians in every sphere of life.
  • Indians were denied high-ranking positions in the administration and army.
  • They were subjected to discriminatory laws and treatment in public spaces.
  • This racial discrimination created a sense of humiliation and injustice among Indians, contributing to their resentment against British rule.

(ii.)

  • The British government imposed taxes on religious places like temples and mosques.
  • This was seen as a direct attack on the religious sentiments of the people.
  • It caused widespread anger and resentment among both Hindus and Muslims.
  • People felt that the British were deliberately trying to undermine their religions and plunder their wealth.

(iii.)

  • The 'Law of Property' affected those who converted to Christianity.
  • According to this law, a convert was allowed to inherit ancestral property.
  • This encouraged conversion and was seen as an attempt to undermine traditional family structures and religious laws.
  • It generated anger as people saw it as another way of interfering with their customs and beliefs.
questions.section_ref: Socio-Religious Causes

questions.question_text:
Socio-religious factors, including missionary activities and discrimination, played a crucial role in the outbreak of the 1857 revolt.

(i.) Explain the socio-religious implications of the 'General Service Enlistment Act' for the sepoys. [3]

(ii.) How did the perceived corruption in the British administration contribute to the socio-religious causes of the revolt? [4]

(iii.) In what way did the oppression of the poor and land taxation contribute to the socio-religious discontent? [3]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • Although the act itself was a military cause, it had socio-religious implications.
  • The 'General Service Enlistment Act' required sepoys to serve overseas, which was against the religious beliefs of many high-caste Hindus who considered sea voyage a loss of caste.
  • This act created a fear of forced conversion and social ostracism among the sepoys.
  • Therefore, a military act also significantly impacted the socio-religious sentiments of sepoys.

(ii.)

  • Corruption was rampant in the British administration, leading to exploitation and injustice.
  • Indian religious leaders and priests were often targeted by corrupt officials for bribes.
  • This created a perception that the British were insensitive and disrespectful towards their religious figures and beliefs.
  • The overall corruption in the administration fueled a sense of resentment and injustice, contributing to the socio-religious causes of the revolt.

(iii.)

  • The oppression of the poor, coupled with high land taxation, led to socio-religious discontent.
  • Many poor farmers were forced to abandon their traditional occupations and convert to other religions for survival.
  • This economic hardship intertwined with a fear of losing their cultural identity, creating a sense of socio-religious crisis.
questions.section_ref: Socio-Religious Causes

questions.question_text:
The economic policies of the British East India Company had a devastating impact on the Indian economy, contributing significantly to the 1857 revolt.

(i.) State three ways in which the exploitation of economic resources by the British impoverished India. [3]

(ii.) Explain how the decay of cottage industries and handicrafts contributed to the uprising of 1857. [3]

(iii.) Describe the impact of the economic decline of the peasantry on the First War of Independence. [4]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • The British extracted raw materials from India at very low prices, depriving Indian producers of fair value.
  • Heavy taxation policies imposed by the British drained wealth from the Indian economy.
  • The British imposed restrictions on Indian industries to protect their own, hindering economic growth.

(ii.)

  • British policies led to the decline of Indian cottage industries and handicrafts.
  • This resulted in widespread unemployment and poverty among artisans.
  • Discontented artisans, deprived of their livelihoods, actively participated in the 1857 revolt.

(iii.)

  • The peasantry suffered heavily due to high land revenue demands and oppressive policies.
  • Many peasants lost their land and became landless labourers.
  • This created widespread resentment and anger towards the British.
  • The impoverished peasantry formed a significant part of the rebel forces in 1857, driven to desperation by economic hardship.
questions.section_ref: Economic Causes

questions.question_text:
The Drain of Wealth and the impoverishment of the Indian people were significant factors that contributed to the First War of Independence.

(i.) What is meant by the 'Drain of Wealth'? [3]

(ii.) State three consequences of the 'Drain of Wealth' on the Indian economy. [3]

(iii.) How did poverty and famines contribute to the widespread discontent leading to the 1857 revolt? [4]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • 'Drain of Wealth' refers to the transfer of wealth from India to Britain for which India did not get an adequate economic return.
  • It included salaries and pensions of British officials, interest on public debt, profits from British investments, and the cost of military expenses borne by India.
  • This drained resources from the Indian economy, hindering its development.

(ii.)

  • It led to a shortage of capital for investment in Indian industries.
  • It resulted in the decline of traditional Indian handicrafts and industries.
  • It increased poverty and economic hardship among the Indian population.

(iii.)

  • Frequent famines caused widespread starvation and death among the Indian population.
  • The British government's inadequate response to famines further fueled resentment.
  • Poverty created desperation and a willingness to revolt against the British rule, as people had nothing to lose.
  • The combination of economic hardship and perceived indifference of the British government pushed many Indians to join the uprising.
questions.section_ref: Economic Causes

questions.question_text:
The economic policies of the British deeply affected various sections of Indian society, ultimately contributing to the revolt of 1857.

(i.) Explain how the British policies led to growing unemployment in India. [3]

(ii.) Describe the condition of Indigo cultivators under British rule. [3]

(iii.) How did the decline of the landed aristocracy serve as an economic cause for the revolt of 1857? [4]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • The destruction of Indian handicrafts and cottage industries led to widespread unemployment, as artisans lost their source of livelihood.
  • The British favored imported goods over Indian products, further reducing employment opportunities.
  • The introduction of new land revenue systems displaced many agricultural workers, adding to the unemployment problem.

(ii.)

  • Indigo cultivators were forced to grow indigo instead of food crops, often at prices dictated by the British planters.
  • They were subjected to oppressive treatment, exploitation, and forced labor.
  • They were often trapped in debt cycles, making it impossible to escape the system.

(iii.)

  • The British land policies, such as the Permanent Settlement, led to the decline of many zamindars (landlords).
  • These dispossessed zamindars lost their land and power, creating resentment towards the British.
  • The decline of the landed aristocracy meant a loss of traditional leadership and influence, leading to social and economic instability.
  • Many dispossessed landlords played a leading role in the revolt, using their resources and influence to mobilize support.
questions.section_ref: Economic Causes

questions.question_text:
The British army, while a source of livelihood for many Indians, also harbored deep-seated grievances. Examine the following military causes that contributed to the 1857 revolt.

(i.) State three ways in which Indian soldiers were ill-treated by the British officers. [3]

(ii.) Explain how the General Service Enlistment Act fueled resentment amongst the sepoys. [3]

(iii.) What effect did the 'faulty distribution of troops' have on the outbreak and spread of the revolt? [4]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • Indian soldiers were treated with contempt and subjected to racial abuse by British officers.
  • They were given low salaries and poor prospects for promotion compared to their British counterparts.
  • They were often required to perform menial tasks that were considered beneath their dignity.

(ii.)

  • The General Service Enlistment Act of 1856 required all new recruits to serve overseas if ordered.
  • This was a violation of the religious beliefs of many high-caste Hindus, who were forbidden from crossing the sea.
  • The act caused widespread resentment among the sepoys, who saw it as a deliberate attempt to undermine their caste and religion.

(iii.)

  • The British had strategically placed most of their European troops in the areas they considered more important or sensitive, such as Bengal and Punjab.
  • This left other regions of India with fewer European soldiers and a larger proportion of Indian sepoys.
  • This created an opportunity for the sepoys in these regions to revolt, knowing that they outnumbered the British troops.
  • The lack of British presence in some areas allowed the revolt to spread more easily, as there was less immediate suppression.

blocks.label: Explanation of General Service Enlistment Act resentment
Effect of faulty distribution of troops

questions.section_ref: Military Causes > Faulty Distribution of Troops

questions.question_text:
The British East India Company's military policies greatly affected the sepoys. With this in mind, answer the following questions:

(i.) Explain two reasons why the prospects for promotion were bleak for Indian soldiers in the Company's army. [3]

(ii.) What were some of the allowances deprived from the Indian sepoys? [3]

(iii.) How did lower salaries for Indian soldiers compared to their British counterparts contribute to the outbreak of the revolt of 1857? [4]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • The highest rank an Indian soldier could achieve was that of a Subedar.
  • All higher ranks were reserved for British officers.
  • There was very little scope for Indian soldiers to rise through the ranks based on merit or experience.

(ii.)

  • Indian sepoys were deprived of extra allowances (Bhatta) when serving in areas away from their homes.
  • They were also deprived of allowances that were given for purchasing rations.
  • The withdrawal of these allowances was perceived as discriminatory and unfair.

(iii.)

  • Indian soldiers received significantly lower salaries compared to their British counterparts, despite performing similar duties.
  • This created a sense of injustice and resentment among the Indian soldiers, who felt undervalued and exploited.
  • The low pay made it difficult for them to support their families, leading to economic hardship and discontent.
  • This economic hardship, combined with other grievances, fueled the anger and resentment that ultimately contributed to the outbreak of the 1857 revolt.
questions.section_ref: Military Causes > Lower Salaries

questions.question_text:
The structure of the British Indian Army was a complex mix of British and Indian soldiers. Examine how the following factors contributed to the 1857 revolt:

(i.) Describe the impact of a larger proportion of Indians in the British Army. [3]

(ii.) Briefly explain the impact of the 'poor performance of British troops' on the Indian sepoys. [3]

(iii.) How did the British officers’ negative perception of the Indian soldiers’ fighting abilities affect their attitude and treatment of them? [4]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • The large number of Indian soldiers meant that the British relied heavily on them to maintain control over India.
  • This created a potential vulnerability, as a widespread revolt among the Indian soldiers could cripple the British administration.
  • The feeling of unity and shared grievances among the Indian soldiers was facilitated by their large numbers, making it easier to coordinate a revolt.

(ii.)

  • The British army's performance during the Afghan wars and the Crimean War was not impressive.
  • These defeats showed the Indian sepoys that the British were not invincible.
  • It boosted the morale of the Indian soldiers and encouraged them to challenge British authority.

(iii.)

  • British officers often viewed Indian soldiers as inferior fighters compared to their European counterparts.
  • This perception led to a lack of respect and appreciation for the Indian soldiers' skills and contributions.
  • It resulted in discriminatory practices and ill-treatment, further fueling resentment and discontent among the Indian soldiers.
  • This negative perception ultimately contributed to the sepoys' desire to rebel and prove their worth.

blocks.label: Impact of negative perception of Indian soldiers

questions.section_ref: Military Causes > Larger Proportion of Indians in the British Army

questions.question_text:
The Revolt of 1857 saw widespread uprisings across India. Examining specific events sheds light on the intensity and character of the revolt.

(i.) Describe the events that unfolded at Meerut, sparking the uprising. [3]

(ii.) Outline the significance of the capture of Delhi by the sepoys. [3]

(iii.) Elaborate on the role and impact of the uprising in Lucknow. [4]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • The revolt at Meerut started with the refusal of sepoys to use the new cartridges greased with animal fat.
  • 85 sepoys were imprisoned for disobedience on May 9, 1857.
  • On May 10, the sepoys broke open the jail, released their comrades, and killed British officers, marking the beginning of the revolt.

(ii.)

  • The capture of Delhi provided the sepoys with a symbolic center.
  • Bahadur Shah II was proclaimed the Emperor of India, legitimizing the revolt.
  • Delhi became the focal point of the rebellion, attracting rebels from other regions and boosting morale.

(iii.)

  • The revolt in Lucknow was led by Begum Hazrat Mahal, who placed her son Birjis Qadr on the throne.
  • The British Residency in Lucknow was besieged for several months, highlighting the intensity of the resistance.
  • The revolt in Lucknow saw significant participation from the local population, including peasants and taluqdars.
  • The prolonged resistance in Lucknow tied down British forces and hampered their efforts to suppress the revolt elsewhere.
questions.section_ref: MAIN EVENTS > Events at Meerut

questions.question_text:
The 1857 revolt was a complex and widespread event. Focusing on specific locations helps in understanding the dynamics of the uprising.

(i.) Briefly describe the key events that transpired in Kanpur during the revolt. [2]

(ii.) Explain the significance of the events in Central India, particularly Jhansi and Gwalior. [4]

(iii.) What role did other centers play in the overall context of the 1857 uprising? Name two such centers. [4]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • Nana Saheb led the revolt in Kanpur.
  • The British garrison at Kanpur was besieged and surrendered.

(ii.)

  • Rani Lakshmibai of Jhansi became a prominent leader after the British refused to recognize her adopted son as heir.
  • She fought bravely against the British, becoming a symbol of resistance.
  • Tatya Tope joined Rani Lakshmibai, providing crucial military support.
  • The capture and subsequent loss of Gwalior was a turning point, leading to the defeat of the rebels in Central India.

(iii.)

  • Other centers, such as Bareilly and Faizabad, played a crucial role in sustaining the revolt.
  • Maulvi Ahmadullah of Faizabad was a key leader who organized resistance in Awadh.
  • Kunwar Singh led the revolt in Bihar, diverting British forces and expanding the geographical scope of the uprising.
  • These centers provided manpower, resources, and strategic support, prolonging the struggle against the British.
questions.section_ref: MAIN EVENTS > Events at Other Centres

questions.question_text:
The First War of Independence of 1857 had profound and lasting consequences that reshaped the political landscape of India.

(i.) State three significant changes introduced in the administration following the war. [3]

(ii.) Explain the main points of Queen Victoria's Proclamation. [3]

(iii.) Describe how the British altered their relationship with the Princely States after the war. [4]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • End of Company Rule: The rule of the East India Company was abolished, and the British Crown assumed direct control over India.
  • Policy of Divide and Rule: The British adopted a deliberate policy of divide and rule to prevent future united uprisings.
  • Changes in Foreign Policy: The British government started to interfere less with the socio religious customs of the country.

(ii.)

  • Assumption of Government: The British Crown formally assumed the government of India.
  • Respect for Indian Customs: The Proclamation promised to respect the rights, dignity, and customs of native princes and people.
  • Equal Opportunity: It assured Indians of equal opportunity for public service, irrespective of race or creed.

(iii.)

  • Policy of Alliance: The British adopted a policy of alliance and cooperation with the princely states.
  • Recognition of Rights: The rights of the princely states were recognized, and their territories were guaranteed against annexation.
  • Dependence on British Power: The princely states were made dependent on the British power, and they were expected to remain loyal to the Crown.
  • Loss of Sovereignty: The states lost most of their sovereignty and had to accept the British Paramountcy.
questions.section_ref: CONSEQUENCES OF THE FIRST WAR OF INDEPENDENCE

questions.question_text:
The First War of Independence brought about significant alterations in the structure and policies of the British government in India.

(i.) State three significant changes that occurred within the army after the 1857 revolt. [3]

(ii.) Explain the meaning and implications of the 'Policy of Divide and Rule' adopted by the British. [4]

(iii.) What impact did the war have on the economic exploitation of India by the British? [3]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • Increased British Troops: The number of British soldiers in the army was increased to maintain control.
  • Reduced Indian Troops: The proportion of Indian soldiers was reduced to prevent future mutinies.
  • Discrimination: Superior positions in the army were reserved for the British.

(ii.)

  • Meaning: The 'Policy of Divide and Rule' involved exacerbating differences between various religious, caste, and ethnic groups in India.
  • Aim: The aim was to prevent any unified front against British rule.
  • Implementation: This policy was implemented by favoring certain groups while suppressing others.
  • Consequence: It fueled communalism and social divisions in Indian society.

(iii.)

  • Increased Exploitation: Economic exploitation continued, but with greater efficiency and control.
  • Raw Materials: India became a major supplier of raw materials to Britain.
  • Finished Goods: The Indian market was flooded with British manufactured goods, hindering local industries.
questions.section_ref: CONSEQUENCES OF THE FIRST WAR OF INDEPENDENCE

questions.question_text:
The British policies of expansion were a significant factor in the 1857 revolt. These policies created resentment and fear among Indian rulers and the general population.

(i.) How did the British expand their power through outright wars? [3]

(ii.) Explain how the Subsidiary Alliance system led to British control over Indian states. [3]

(iii.) What were the main principles of the Doctrine of Lapse, and how did it contribute to the uprising of 1857? [4]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • The British engaged in outright wars to annex territories like Sindh and Punjab.
  • These wars demonstrated British military power and a disregard for Indian sovereignty.
  • The annexation of these territories caused resentment among rulers and soldiers who lost their livelihoods.

(ii.)

  • The Subsidiary Alliance forced Indian rulers to maintain British troops in their territory and pay for their upkeep.
  • It restricted the rulers' ability to form alliances with other powers and conduct independent foreign policy.
  • This system gradually eroded the autonomy and financial stability of Indian states, increasing British influence.

(iii.)

  • The Doctrine of Lapse allowed the British to annex Indian states if a ruler died without a natural heir.
  • It was applied to states like Satara, Jhansi, and Nagpur, causing widespread resentment and anger.
  • The annexation of these states deprived Indian rulers of their power and prestige.
  • It instilled fear among other rulers who worried they could be next, contributing significantly to the uprising of 1857.
section_refs: CAUSES OF THE FIRST WAR OF INDEPENDENCE
Political Causes > Policy of Expansion > By Outright Wars
Political Causes > Policy of Expansion > By Subsidiary Alliance
Political Causes > Policy of Expansion > By Using the Doctrine of Lapse

questions.question_text:
Several factors contributed to the political discontent that fueled the First War of Independence. Disrespect towards rulers and unpopular policies played key roles.

(i.) How was Bahadur Shah Zafar, the Mughal Emperor, treated with disrespect by the British? [3]

(ii.) What specific grievances did Nana Saheb have against the British, and why? [3]

(iii.) Explain how the "absentee sovereignty" of the British East India Company contributed to the political causes of the 1857 revolt. [4]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • The British reduced Bahadur Shah Zafar's pension and privileges.
  • They prohibited him from using his title of Emperor after his death.
  • They announced the removal of the Mughal dynasty from the Red Fort, which deeply offended Muslims.

(ii.)

  • Nana Saheb was the adopted son of Peshwa Baji Rao II.
  • The British refused to grant Nana Saheb his father's pension after Baji Rao II's death.
  • This perceived injustice and denial of his rightful inheritance fueled his resentment towards the British.

(iii.)

  • The British East India Company's headquarters were far away in England, creating a sense of detachment from the governed.
  • This absentee sovereignty led to policies that prioritized British interests over the welfare of the Indian population.
  • Decisions were often made without adequate understanding of local conditions or concerns.
  • This resulted in widespread dissatisfaction and a feeling of being exploited and disregarded by a foreign power, thus contributing to the revolt.
section_refs: CAUSES OF THE FIRST WAR OF INDEPENDENCE
Political Causes > Disrespect Shown to Bahadur Shah
Political Causes > Treatment Given to Nana Saheb
Political Causes > Absentee Sovereignty of the British

questions.question_text:
The British East India Company employed various policies to expand its control over India, often leading to resentment and ultimately contributing to the 1857 revolt.

(i.) How did the British use outright wars to expand their territory in India? [3]

(ii.) Explain the main features of the Subsidiary Alliance system. [3]

(iii.) What role did the Doctrine of Lapse play in the expansionist policies of the British, and state three annexations it caused? [4]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • The British engaged in direct military conflicts to annex territories.
  • Examples include the Anglo-Mysore Wars, resulting in the annexation of Mysore.
  • The Anglo-Maratha Wars further expanded British control over central India.

(ii.)

  • Indian rulers were forced to keep British troops in their territory.
  • The rulers had to pay for the maintenance of these troops.
  • They were restricted from forming alliances with other powers without British consent.
  • A British Resident was stationed at the ruler's court.

(iii.)

  • The Doctrine of Lapse allowed the British to annex states if the ruler died without a natural heir.
  • Satara was annexed in 1848.
  • Nagpur was annexed in 1854.
  • Jhansi was annexed in 1853.
questions.section_ref: The First War of Independence > CAUSES OF THE FIRST WAR OF INDEPENDENCE > Political Causes > Policy of Expansion

questions.question_text:
British expansionist policies under the East India Company were a significant factor leading to the simmering discontent among Indian rulers and the population.

(i.) Describe the key conditions imposed on Indian states under the Subsidiary Alliance. [3]

(ii.) Explain how the British exploited the Doctrine of Lapse to annex Indian territories. [3]

(iii.) What were the consequences of the British annexation of Awadh on the pretext of alleged misrule? [4]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • Indian rulers had to accept British forces within their territories.
  • They were required to pay for the maintenance of these forces.
  • They were forbidden from entering into alliances with other powers without British approval.
  • A British Resident was stationed in their court, effectively controlling their foreign policy.

(ii.)

  • The Doctrine of Lapse stated that if an Indian ruler died without a natural male heir, his kingdom would lapse or be annexed by the British.
  • The British used this to annex states like Satara, Jhansi, and Nagpur.
  • They disregarded the traditional Indian practice of adopting an heir.

(iii.)

  • The annexation of Awadh was widely resented as it was a wealthy and well-administered province.
  • It led to the disbanding of the Awadh army, causing widespread unemployment among soldiers.
  • The annexation also affected the taluqdars (landlords), who lost their lands and privileges.
  • This created widespread discontent among the people of Awadh, who later played a key role in the 1857 revolt.
questions.section_ref: The First War of Independence > CAUSES OF THE FIRST WAR OF INDEPENDENCE > Political Causes > Policy of Expansion

questions.question_text:
The expansionist policies of the British East India Company deeply impacted the political landscape of India, sparking widespread resentment.

(i.) Explain how 'outright wars' led to the expansion of British territories. Give two examples. [3]

(ii.) In what ways did the Subsidiary Alliance system compromise the autonomy of Indian rulers? [3]

(iii.) What were the long-term implications of the British annexations under the Doctrine of Lapse on the stability of the region? [4]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • The British engaged in direct military conquests to expand their territories.
  • Victory in battles allowed them to annex or control states.
  • Examples include the Anglo-Mysore Wars and the Anglo-Sikh Wars.

(ii.)

  • Indian rulers who signed the Subsidiary Alliance had to maintain British troops in their territory.
  • They were required to pay for the upkeep of these troops, draining their resources.
  • They lost the right to form alliances with other powers, making them dependent on the British.

(iii.)

  • The Doctrine of Lapse created a sense of insecurity and distrust among Indian rulers.
  • It led to the annexation of several states, causing widespread resentment.
  • Many rulers felt threatened by the aggressive expansionist policies of the British.
  • The annexations contributed significantly to the build-up of anti-British sentiment, culminating in the revolt of 1857.
questions.section_ref: The First War of Independence > CAUSES OF THE FIRST WAR OF INDEPENDENCE > Political Causes > Policy of Expansion

questions.question_text:
The socio-religious causes were a significant factor in the 1857 revolt. These causes deeply affected the sentiments of the Indian population.

(i.) Explain how interference with social customs contributed to the uprising. [3]

(ii.) Describe the role of missionaries in causing resentment among Indians. [3]

(iii.) How did the Law of Property Act of 1850 impact traditional Indian society and fuel the revolt? [4]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • The British interfered with social customs such as Sati and female infanticide.
  • These reforms, though well-intentioned, were seen as an attack on their traditions.
  • This created resentment and distrust towards British rule.

(ii.)

  • Christian missionaries openly preached Christianity and criticized Hindu and Muslim religions.
  • They established schools and hospitals to spread their faith.
  • Many Indians viewed their activities with suspicion and feared conversion.
  • These activities led to widespread resentment and fear of cultural disruption.

(iii.)

  • The Law of Property Act of 1850 allowed converts to Christianity to inherit ancestral property.
  • This act was viewed as an incentive for conversion and a direct attack on Hindu and Muslim inheritance laws.
  • It created widespread anger and fear among the orthodox sections of the society.
  • The act was seen as a deliberate attempt to undermine their religious beliefs and social structures, contributing to the revolt.
questions.section_ref: Socio-Religious Causes > Law of Property

questions.question_text:
Several socio-religious factors contributed to the widespread discontent leading up to the 1857 revolt, fueling anger against British rule.

(i.) State how the British policy of racial discrimination was a cause of the 1857 revolt. [3]

(ii.) Explain how apprehension about modern innovations added to the resentment. [3]

(iii.) How did the taxing of religious places affect Indian sentiments and contribute to the uprising? [4]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • The British consistently showed racial superiority towards Indians.
  • Indians were excluded from high-ranking positions in the government and army.
  • They were treated with disrespect and contempt by the British.
  • This created a sense of humiliation and resentment among Indians.

(ii.)

  • Many Indians were apprehensive about modern innovations introduced by the British, such as railways and telegraphs.
  • They feared that these innovations would undermine their traditional social structure and culture.
  • Rumors spread that the British were using these innovations to convert Indians to Christianity.

(iii.)

  • The British imposed taxes on lands belonging to temples and mosques.
  • This action deeply hurt the religious sentiments of both Hindus and Muslims.
  • It was seen as a direct attack on their religious institutions.
  • The taxes imposed financial burden on religious institutions, leading to further discontent and resentment against the British.
questions.section_ref: Socio-Religious Causes > Taxing Religious Places

questions.question_text:
The economic policies of the British East India Company had a devastating impact on India's economy and its people, contributing significantly to the First War of Independence.

(i.) State three ways in which the British exploited India's economic resources. [3]

(ii.) Explain three consequences of the decay of cottage industries and handicrafts. [3]

(iii.) Describe four reasons for the economic decline of the peasantry under British rule. [4]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • The British imposed heavy taxes on land, extracting a large portion of the peasants' produce.
  • They monopolized trade, controlling the export of raw materials and the import of finished goods, often at unfavorable terms for India.
  • They exploited India's natural resources, such as minerals and forests, for their own benefit, without adequately compensating the local population or investing in infrastructure.

(ii.)

  • Widespread unemployment resulted, as artisans and craftsmen lost their livelihoods.
  • Increased dependence on agriculture, as people turned to farming as a last resort, putting pressure on land resources.
  • Decline in the quality of life, as many skilled workers were reduced to poverty.

(iii.)

  • High land revenue demands forced many peasants into debt.
  • The British imposed a rigid revenue system, which did not take into account natural calamities such as droughts and floods.
  • The introduction of cash crops at the expense of food crops led to food shortages and increased vulnerability to market fluctuations.
  • The destruction of traditional irrigation systems and the neglect of agriculture by the British further aggravated the plight of the peasantry.
questions.section_ref: Economic Causes > Economic Decline of Peasantry

questions.question_text:
The British East India Company's military policies greatly affected Indian soldiers. These policies were a significant factor leading to the 1857 revolt.

(i.) State three ways in which Indian soldiers were ill-treated by their British superiors. [3]

(ii.) Explain how the General Service Enlistment Act fueled resentment among Indian sepoys. [3]

(iii.) Describe the impact of 'Faulty Distribution of Troops' on the outbreak and spread of the revolt. [4]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • Indian soldiers were often subjected to racial discrimination and verbal abuse by their British officers.
  • They were denied opportunities for promotion to higher ranks, regardless of their experience or competence.
  • Indian sepoys were compelled to serve in regions beyond India without extra compensation, which was against their religious beliefs and customs.

(ii.)

  • The General Service Enlistment Act mandated that all new recruits had to be willing to serve anywhere the Company required, including overseas.
  • This was a direct violation of the religious beliefs of many high-caste Hindus who considered crossing the sea as losing their caste and hence unacceptable.
  • It created widespread fear and resentment among the sepoys, who perceived it as an attempt to undermine their social and religious traditions, fostering distrust.

(iii.)

  • The British deliberately kept the bulk of its European forces in cantonment towns and away from the main population centers.
  • This led to a false sense of security and an inadequate troop presence in strategically important areas, leaving them vulnerable to rebellion.
  • The poor distribution of troops allowed the sepoys to gain initial momentum in the revolt and spread it from one garrison to another.
  • The uneven spread of forces also meant that initial uprisings in some areas could not be quickly suppressed, further emboldening other discontented elements to join the revolt.
questions.section_ref: Military Causes > Faulty Distribution of Troops

questions.question_text:
The First War of Independence saw significant events unfold across India, shaping the course of the uprising against British rule. Examine key events:

(i.) Briefly describe the events that transpired at Meerut, marking the beginning of the uprising. [3]

(ii.) Explain the significance of the events at Delhi during the 1857 revolt. [3]

(iii.) Elaborate on the events that took place in Kanpur, highlighting the key figures and outcomes. [4]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • The uprising at Meerut began with the refusal of sepoys to use the greased cartridges.
  • Eighty-five sepoys were imprisoned for their defiance.
  • On May 10, 1857, the sepoys revolted, freeing their imprisoned comrades and killing British officers.

(ii.)

  • Delhi was a crucial centre as the sepoys marched there from Meerut and declared Bahadur Shah Zafar as their leader.
  • The capture of Delhi provided a symbolic victory and legitimacy to the rebellion.
  • The British initially struggled to regain control of Delhi, highlighting the strength of the rebel forces.

(iii.)

  • The events at Kanpur were centered around Nana Saheb, the adopted son of the late Peshwa Baji Rao II.
  • Nana Saheb initially offered support to the British but later joined the rebels after being denied his pension.
  • The siege of Kanpur resulted in the surrender of the British garrison, followed by the controversial massacre of British women and children at Bibighar.
  • Kanpur was eventually recaptured by the British under General Havelock.
questions.section_ref: MAIN EVENTS > Events at Kanpur

questions.question_text:
The First War of Independence brought about significant changes in the governance and administration of India. Let's examine some of these transformations.

(i.) Briefly describe how the First War of Independence led to the end of the Company's rule. [3]

(ii.) What changes were made in the army after the revolt of 1857? [3]

(iii.) Explain the purpose and significance of Queen Victoria's Proclamation of 1858. [4]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • The First War of Independence exposed the weaknesses and inadequacies of the East India Company's administration.
  • The British government felt that the Company was no longer capable of effectively governing India.
  • The Government of India Act 1858 was passed, transferring power from the East India Company to the British Crown.

(ii.)

  • The proportion of British troops to Indian troops was increased to ensure British dominance.
  • Discrimination on the basis of caste was abolished.
  • The army was reorganized to prevent any future large-scale uprising by Indian soldiers.

(iii.)

  • The purpose of Queen Victoria's Proclamation was to reassure the Indian people after the revolt.
  • It promised no further expansion of British territories in India.
  • It guaranteed religious freedom and equal protection under the law for all Indians.
  • It aimed to win the loyalty of the Indian population and establish a more stable relationship between the British government and its Indian subjects.
questions.section_ref: CONSEQUENCES OF THE FIRST WAR OF INDEPENDENCE

questions.question_text:
The First War of Independence brought about significant shifts in British administration in India. These changes had long-lasting effects on the subcontinent.

(i.) State three ways in which the end of Company rule altered the governance of India. [3]

(ii.) Explain how the policy of 'Divide and Rule' impacted social cohesion in India after 1857. [3]

(iii.) Discuss the changes introduced in the British foreign policy following the revolt. [4]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • End of Dual Government: The revolt led to the abolition of the dual system of governance, transferring power directly to the British Crown.
  • Secretary of State for India: A new office of the Secretary of State for India was created in the British government to oversee Indian affairs.
  • Viceroy: The Governor-General was replaced by the Viceroy, who acted as the direct representative of the British Crown in India.

(ii.)

  • Exploitation of Differences: The British deliberately fostered divisions between different religious and caste groups.
  • Communal Representation: Separate electorates were introduced, granting special representation to minority communities, further solidifying divisions.
  • Weakening of Unity: The policy aimed at weakening the united front displayed during the revolt, preventing future uprisings.

(iii.)

  • Buffer States: The British sought to create buffer states along India's borders to protect against potential threats from Russia and other powers.
  • Friendly Relations: They fostered friendly relationships with neighboring countries, such as Afghanistan, to ensure the security of India.
  • Increased Control: The foreign policy aimed at consolidating British influence in the region and preventing any rival powers from gaining a foothold.
  • Territorial Expansion: While direct annexation slowed, indirect control and influence over surrounding territories increased to secure British interests.
questions.section_ref: CONSEQUENCES OF THE FIRST WAR OF INDEPENDENCE > Changes in Administration

questions.question_text:
The aftermath of the 1857 revolt saw substantial alterations in the British approach to India. These changes deeply influenced the future of the subcontinent.

(i.) Detail two ways in which racial antagonism manifested itself in India after the First War of Independence. [2]

(ii.) Elaborate on the religious changes or the absence thereof, brought about by the British after the 1857 revolt. [4]

(iii.) Analyze the economic exploitation perpetuated by the British even after the First War of Independence. [4]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • Social Segregation: Increased segregation of residential areas and clubs, excluding Indians from spaces occupied by the British.
  • Discrimination in Employment: Despite promises of equality, discrimination in higher-level government jobs continued, favoring the British.

(ii.)

  • Policy of Non-Interference: The British adopted a policy of non-interference in religious matters, reversing earlier attempts at social reform.
  • Maintenance of Status Quo: Existing religious laws and customs were largely maintained to avoid causing further unrest.
  • Limited Reforms: While direct intervention ceased, subtle influences through education and administration continued to impact religious practices.
  • No Overt Conversions: The government distanced itself from overt missionary activities to avoid alienating the Indian population.

(iii.)

  • Continued Drain of Wealth: The economic drain of wealth from India to Britain persisted through various mechanisms like home charges and unequal trade.
  • Exploitation of Resources: British industries continued to exploit Indian raw materials for their benefit, often at the expense of local producers.
  • Heavy Taxation: Peasants and landowners faced heavy taxation policies, contributing to widespread poverty and economic hardship.
  • Suppression of Indian Industries: Policies that favored British manufactured goods over Indian handicrafts further crippled the local economy, leading to unemployment and economic decline.
questions.section_ref: CONSEQUENCES OF THE FIRST WAR OF INDEPENDENCE > Changes in Administration

questions.question_text:
Queen Victoria's Proclamation attempted to assuage the concerns of Indians after the 1857 uprising. However, the implementation of these promises was complex and uneven.

(i.) Mention three key promises made in Queen Victoria’s Proclamation of 1858. [3]

(ii.) How did the relationships with the Princely States change after the First War of Independence, and why? [3]

(iii.) Describe the significant changes that were made in the structure and composition of the British Army in India after 1857. [4]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • Religious Freedom: Guaranteed religious freedom and non-interference in religious matters.
  • Equal Protection under the Law: Promised equal protection under the law for all citizens, regardless of race or creed.
  • No Further Annexation: Assured that there would be no further annexation of Indian states.

(ii.)

  • Recognition of Rights: The British recognized the rights and dignity of the Princely States, ensuring their continued existence.
  • Alliance and Control: The states were brought under British paramountcy, becoming allies but also subject to British control.
  • Buffer Zones: They served as buffer zones to prevent future large-scale uprisings and to maintain stability.

(iii.)

  • Increased British Troops: The proportion of British troops in the army was increased to maintain control and prevent mutinies.
  • Arms Control: Artillery and other strategic arms were placed exclusively in the hands of British troops.
  • Regimental Reorganization: Regiments were reorganized based on caste and community lines to prevent unified action and foster internal divisions.
  • Appointment of Commission: A commission was appointed to investigate and reorganize the Indian army after the revolt.

CONSEQUENCES OF THE FIRST WAR OF INDEPENDENCE > Relations with Princely States
CONSEQUENCES OF THE FIRST WAR OF INDEPENDENCE > Changes in the Army

questions.question_text:
The British employed various policies to expand their control in India, often leading to resentment and ultimately contributing to the 1857 revolt.

(i.) Briefly describe the 'Doctrine of Lapse' and name two states annexed using it. [3]

(ii.) Explain the 'Subsidiary Alliance' system. What were its key features? [3]

(iii.) Detail how the British used the pretext of 'alleged misrule' to annex territories. Give one example. [4]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • The Doctrine of Lapse was a policy where if an Indian ruler died without a natural male heir, his kingdom would be annexed by the British.
  • Satara was annexed in 1848.
  • Nagpur was annexed in 1854.

(ii.)

  • The Subsidiary Alliance was a system where Indian rulers were forced to keep British troops in their territory.
  • Indian rulers had to pay for the maintenance of these troops.
  • They were also forbidden from entering into alliances with other powers or declaring war without the permission of the British.

(iii.)

  • The British frequently annexed territories under the pretext of "alleged misrule", claiming the ruler was incompetent or corrupt and the people were suffering.
  • This was often used to justify annexations when other policies like the Doctrine of Lapse didn't apply.
  • Oudh was annexed in 1856 on the grounds of alleged misgovernment, despite its long-standing loyalty to the British.
  • This annexation caused widespread resentment, as it was seen as a betrayal.
questions.section_ref: The First War of Independence > CAUSES OF THE FIRST WAR OF INDEPENDENCE > Political Causes > Policy of Expansion > On the Pretext of Alleged Misrule

questions.question_text:
The political landscape of mid-19th century India was turbulent, marked by British expansionist policies and disregard for Indian rulers and traditions. These factors fueled resentment and ultimately contributed to the First War of Independence.

(i.) Explain how the Doctrine of Lapse contributed to the political causes of the 1857 Revolt. [3]

(ii.) How did the treatment meted out to Nana Saheb exacerbate the simmering discontent among the Indian populace? [3]

(iii.) Discuss the political impact of the British policy of outright wars with reference to the First War of Independence. [4]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • The Doctrine of Lapse was an annexation policy.
  • It allowed the British to annex states where the ruler died without a natural heir.
  • This created insecurity and resentment among Indian rulers who feared losing their kingdoms, thus leading to political instability and anti-British sentiment.

(ii.)

  • Nana Saheb was the adopted son of Peshwa Baji Rao II.
  • After the Peshwa's death, the British refused to continue his pension.
  • This act was seen as unjust and disrespectful, causing widespread anger and resentment among the Maratha population and others who sympathized with Nana Saheb.
  • It highlighted the perceived greed and insensitivity of the British.

(iii.)

  • The British engaged in outright wars to expand their territory, such as the Anglo-Sikh Wars and the annexation of Sindh.
  • These wars demonstrated British military power and ruthlessness.
  • They instilled fear and resentment among the Indian population and other rulers who viewed the British as aggressive and expansionist.
  • The annexation of territories through wars led to the displacement of local rulers and officials, causing political instability and contributing to the overall resentment that fueled the 1857 Revolt.
questions.section_ref: The First War of Independence/CAUSES OF THE FIRST WAR OF INDEPENDENCE/Political Causes

questions.question_text:
Study the image.

(i.) What figure is shown in this image? Give one detail about his position in 1857. [3]

(ii.) Explain how the British showed disrespect to this figure. [3]

(iii.) What consequence did this disrespect have in relation to the revolt of 1857? [4]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • The image shows Bahadur Shah Zafar.
  • He was the last Mughal Emperor of India in 1857.
  • Bahadur Shah Zafar was a symbolic leader for the Indian soldiers.

(ii.)

  • Lord Dalhousie announced in 1849 that Bahadur Shah Zafar's successors would have to leave the Red Fort.
  • Lord Canning announced in 1856 that Bahadur Shah Zafar would be the last Mughal King and his successors would only be princes.
  • This clearly undermined the emperor's authority and humiliated him and his family.

(iii.)

  • The disrespect shown to Bahadur Shah Zafar angered many Indians who still held the Mughal dynasty in high regard.
  • It was a key political cause of the 1857 revolt.
  • This disrespect generated widespread resentment and contributed significantly to the anti-British sentiment that fueled the uprising.
  • Many Indians saw Bahadur Shah Zafar as the only leader who could unite them and free India from British rule.

questions.question_text:
Study the image.

(i.) Who is the person in the image? Describe his position at the time of the 1857 revolt. [3]

(ii.) Briefly explain the significance of Delhi as a centre of the revolt with reference to this person. [3]

(iii.) What was the consequence for this person following the British recapture of Delhi? What significance did this have for the future of India? [4]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • The person in the image is Bahadur Shah Zafar.
  • He was the last Mughal Emperor of India.
  • During the revolt of 1857, he was a largely symbolic figurehead for the rebels in Delhi.

(ii.)

  • Delhi held immense historical and symbolic significance as the former capital of the Mughal Empire.
  • The rebels declared Bahadur Shah Zafar as their leader, hoping to unite Indians under the Mughal banner.
  • The presence of the Mughal Emperor in Delhi gave legitimacy and a focal point to the revolt.

(iii.)

  • Following the recapture of Delhi by the British, Bahadur Shah Zafar was arrested and exiled to Rangoon (Burma).
  • This exile marked the formal end of the Mughal dynasty, which had ruled India for centuries.
  • The end of the Mughal dynasty removed a potential rallying point for future resistance movements.
  • The British victory at Delhi and the exile of Bahadur Shah Zafar solidified British control and ushered in a new era of direct British rule.
