questions.question_text:
Study the image.

(i.) Who is the person in the image? State his relationship to the last Peshwa. [3]

(ii.) Explain the reason for the British East India Company's actions against Nana Saheb. [3]

(iii.) What was the ultimate consequence of the treatment meted out to Nana Saheb in the context of the 1857 revolt? [4]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • The person in the image is Nana Saheb.
  • He was the adopted son of the last Peshwa, Baji Rao II.

(ii.)

  • The British East India Company refused to grant Nana Saheb the pension that was being paid to Baji Rao II.
  • This refusal was based on the fact that Nana Saheb was an adopted son and not a natural heir.
  • This denial of pension caused great resentment and anger in Nana Saheb.

(iii.)

  • The ill-treatment and denial of pension to Nana Saheb made him a staunch enemy of the British.
  • He became one of the key leaders of the 1857 revolt, particularly in the Kanpur region.
  • His participation in the revolt, fueled by his grievances, significantly intensified the uprising and posed a serious challenge to British rule.
  • His involvement gave the revolt both legitimacy and military leadership in the Kanpur area.

Correctly states his relationship to Baji Rao II.
Mentions the denial of the pension.

questions.question_text:
Study the image.

(i.) Identify the figure in the image. Briefly describe her role in the events at Lucknow during the 1857 revolt. [3]

(ii.) Explain the immediate circumstances that led to the uprising in Lucknow, connecting it to broader causes of the revolt. [3]

(iii.) Discuss the significance of the events at Lucknow in the context of the entire First War of Independence, focusing on its impact and consequences. [4]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • The figure is Begum Hazrat Mahal.
  • She was the wife of the exiled Nawab Wajid Ali Shah.
  • She took charge of Lucknow after the revolt broke out and played a key role in the uprising against the British.

(ii.)

  • The annexation of Awadh in 1856 by the British was a key factor.
  • This created widespread resentment among the people of Lucknow.
  • Rumors about greased cartridges further fueled the anger, leading to open rebellion, with soldiers and civilians joining forces against the British.
  • The deposition of her son was another factor.

(iii.)

  • The events at Lucknow demonstrated the widespread resentment towards British rule.
  • The revolt in Lucknow was one of the most prolonged and intense conflicts.
  • Begum Hazrat Mahal's leadership inspired resistance.
  • Lucknow's resistance tied up British forces, impacting the overall course of the war and showing that the revolt was not just a sepoy mutiny, but a broader popular uprising.

Mentions annexation of Awadh (or Wajid Ali Shah's exile).

Mentions intensity/prolonged nature of conflict there.

Explains that it shows the war wasn't just a mutiny.

questions.question_text:
Study the image.

(i.) What does this image primarily represent, in the context of the 1857 revolt? [3]

(ii.) Briefly explain any two specific socio-religious causes that contributed to the depicted situation. [3]

(iii.) How did the economic policies of the British contribute to the widespread resentment shown in the image? [4]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • The image represents the widespread discontent and uprising against the British East India Company in 1857.
  • It depicts the unified rebellion of sepoys, peasants, and rulers, against foreign rule.
  • The picture highlights the broad participation of different sections of Indian society in the revolt.

(ii.)

  • Interference with Social Customs: The British tried to abolish practices like Sati and child marriage, which were seen as interference.
  • Activities of Missionaries: Christian missionaries attempted to convert people to Christianity, sparking fears of religious conversion.
  • Policy of Racial Discrimination: Indians were subjected to racial prejudice and were considered inferior to the British.

(iii.)

  • Exploitation of Economic Resources: The British exploited India's resources for their own benefit, leading to economic hardship.
  • Decay of Cottage Industries: British policies destroyed local industries, making artisans and craftsmen unemployed.
  • Economic Decline of Peasantry: High taxes and revenue demands impoverished the farmers, pushing them into debt and desperation.
  • Drain of Wealth: The British drained wealth from India to Britain, impoverishing the Indian economy.

criteria.description: Identifies the image as representing the widespread uprising.
Mentions the rebellion against the British East India Company.
Indicates broad participation.
Identifies one socio-religious cause accurately.
Explains the cause clearly, showing relevance to the revolt.

questions.question_text:
Study the image.

(i.) What does this image depict regarding the composition of the British army in India? [3]

(ii.) Explain how the large proportion of Indian soldiers in the British army contributed to the outbreak of the 1857 revolt. [3]

(iii.) What other military grievances, apart from numerical strength, fueled the resentment of Indian sepoys, ultimately leading to the 1857 uprising? [4]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • The image shows Indian sepoys serving alongside British officers or soldiers in the British East India Company's army.
  • It illustrates the significant presence and reliance on Indian soldiers within the British military structure in India.
  • It suggests a disparity in roles or ranks between British and Indian personnel.

(ii.)

  • The large number of Indian soldiers meant that any widespread discontent among them had the potential to cripple the British army.
  • Their collective grievances, such as discrimination and poor treatment, could easily translate into a large-scale rebellion.
  • The sepoys felt a sense of unity and shared identity, facilitating coordinated action against the British.

(iii.)

  • Ill-treatment of Indian soldiers was a major grievance. They faced racial discrimination and abuse from British officers.
  • The General Service Enlistment Act, which required sepoys to serve anywhere, including overseas, violated their religious beliefs.
  • There were bleak prospects for promotion for Indian soldiers. They were denied high ranks and positions of authority.
  • Deprivation of allowances (Bhatta) in certain regions further fueled resentment.

questions.question_text:
Study the image.

(i.) Briefly describe the image. [3]

(ii.) Explain how the person in the image used the 'Doctrine of Lapse'. [3]

(iii.) What were the consequences of the 'Doctrine of Lapse' in relation to the 1857 revolt? [4]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • The image shows Lord Dalhousie, who was the Governor-General of India.
  • He is portrayed as a key figure in the expansionist policies of the British East India Company.
  • His tenure is closely associated with the Doctrine of Lapse.

(ii.)

  • Lord Dalhousie implemented the Doctrine of Lapse as a policy to annex states.
  • If a ruler of a dependent state died without a natural heir, the state would be annexed by the British.
  • This policy was used to acquire territories like Satara, Nagpur, and Jhansi.

(iii.)

  • The Doctrine of Lapse created resentment and distrust among Indian rulers.
  • It fueled anger towards the British East India Company.
  • The annexation of states through this doctrine was a major political cause of the 1857 revolt, as rulers felt threatened and dispossessed.
  • For example, the annexation of Jhansi led to Rani Lakshmibai becoming a prominent leader in the revolt.

questions.question_text:
Study the image.

(i.) Identify the historical figure in the image. [3]

(ii.) Explain the policy associated with this figure regarding the annexation of Indian states based on alleged misrule. [3]

(iii.) Discuss the impact of this annexation policy as a cause of the 1857 Revolt. [4]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • The historical figure in the image is Lord Dalhousie.
  • He served as the Governor-General of India.
  • He is best known for his policies of annexation.

(ii.)

  • Lord Dalhousie implemented a policy of annexing Indian states on the pretext of 'alleged misrule'.
  • This meant that if Dalhousie believed a ruler was incompetent or mismanaging their state, he would annex it.
  • Awadh was annexed using this policy.

(iii.)

  • The annexation of Awadh on the pretext of misrule was a significant cause of the 1857 Revolt.
  • It caused widespread resentment and anger among the people of Awadh.
  • Many sepoys came from Awadh; the annexation directly affected their families and land holdings.
  • The annexation contributed to the feeling that the British were disregarding Indian traditions and governance.

questions.question_text:
Study the image.

(i.) Identify the policy of expansion depicted and name one specific region or state acquired through outright war by the British. [3]

(ii.) Explain how the British policy of expansion through outright wars contributed to resentment among Indian rulers and the general population. [3]

(iii.) Discuss the significance of these territorial annexations as a cause of the First War of Independence in 1857. [4]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • The image depicts the British policy of expansion through outright wars.
  • Examples of regions acquired through outright wars include Sindh, Punjab, and parts of Mysore.

(ii.)

  • Outright wars led to the forceful annexation of Indian states, disregarding the rights and sovereignty of Indian rulers.
  • This created a sense of injustice and humiliation among the ruling classes.
  • The loss of territories deprived many people of their livelihoods and positions, fueling widespread resentment.

(iii.)

  • The policy of expansion by outright wars demonstrated the British East India Company's aggressive intentions and disregard for Indian autonomy.
  • The annexation of key states like Punjab created a climate of fear and distrust.
  • The displacement of rulers and their armies resulted in a large number of unemployed soldiers and officials, who then became active participants in the revolt of 1857.
  • The cumulative effect of these annexations contributed significantly to the growing anti-British sentiment that triggered the uprising.

questions.question_text:
Study the image relating to the Subsidiary Alliance.

(i.) Identify what the Subsidiary Alliance entailed for Indian rulers. [3]

(ii.) Explain why the Subsidiary Alliance was a significant political cause of the 1857 Revolt. [3]

(iii.) What consequences did the implementation of the Subsidiary Alliance have on the relationship between the British and the Indian states? [4]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • Indian rulers accepting the Subsidiary Alliance had to keep British troops in their territory.
  • They had to pay for the maintenance of these troops.
  • They were restricted from having independent foreign relations.

(ii.)

  • The Subsidiary Alliance led to the annexation of several Indian states.
  • It caused resentment among Indian rulers who lost their power and autonomy.
  • It created a sense of humiliation and anger towards the British.

(iii.)

  • It led to the weakening of Indian states' military strength.
  • It increased British control over Indian territories and resources.
  • It fostered a sense of distrust and resentment between the British and Indian rulers.
  • It ultimately contributed to the growing discontent that culminated in the 1857 Revolt.

questions.question_text:
Study the image.

(i.) What entity is referenced when we say "the Company's Rule" in the context of pre-1857 India? Describe its administrative structure. [3]

(ii.) Explain briefly the main reasons that led to the end of the Company's Rule in India after the revolt of 1857. [3]

(iii.) What were the key changes in administration that took place immediately after the end of the Company's Rule as a direct consequence of the 1857 revolt? [4]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • "The Company's Rule" refers to the British East India Company.
  • It was a private trading company that gradually gained political and administrative control over large parts of India.
  • Its administrative structure involved a Governor-General, a Board of Directors in London, and a civil service to administer the territories.

(ii.)

  • The First War of Independence exposed the weaknesses of the Company's administration.
  • The British government felt that the Company was no longer capable of effectively governing India.
  • There was widespread public opinion in Britain favoring direct rule to restore stability and prevent future uprisings.

(iii.)

  • The British Crown assumed direct control of India.
  • The Governor-General was replaced by the Viceroy, who was the direct representative of the Crown.
  • The Board of Control and Court of Directors of the Company were abolished.
  • The India Office was established in London to oversee Indian affairs, headed by the Secretary of State for India.

questions.question_text:
Study the image.

(i.) Briefly describe what this image likely depicts in the context of the chapter. [3]

(ii.) Explain the meaning and aims of the British policy of 'Divide and Rule'. [3]

(iii.) What were the consequences of this policy in India after the 1857 revolt? [4]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • The image most likely shows the British attempting to create divisions between different religious or social groups in India.
  • It could depict preferential treatment to some groups.
  • It may involve inciting rivalry between Hindus and Muslims or other communities.

(ii.)

  • The policy of Divide and Rule aimed to maintain British control over India.
  • It involved creating or exacerbating divisions based on religion, caste, or region among the Indian population.
  • The British aimed to prevent a united front against their rule by fostering distrust and animosity between different groups.

(iii.)

  • The policy led to increased communal tensions and mistrust among different communities.
  • It hindered the development of a unified national identity.
  • It exacerbated existing social and religious differences, contributing to long-term social fragmentation.
  • The policy strengthened British control by making it harder for Indians to unite against them.

questions.question_text:
Study the image.

(i.) What does the image depict in the context of Racial Antagonism after 1857? [3]

(ii.) Explain how the events of 1857 contributed to the intensification of Racial Antagonism in India. [3]

(iii.) What were the long-term consequences of this increased Racial Antagonism on British administration and the Indian population? [4]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • The image depicts a situation of racial discrimination, showcasing unequal treatment and segregation between the British and the Indians.
  • It suggests a power imbalance, with the British holding a superior position.
  • The image likely shows visible signs of segregation or biased behaviour, emphasizing the divide after 1857.

(ii.)

  • The 1857 revolt, being a direct challenge to British authority, instilled fear and resentment among the British towards the Indian population.
  • The British saw the uprising as a sign of Indian 'untrustworthiness' and 'barbarism', leading to increased prejudice.
  • This fear led to the implementation of policies designed to keep Indians subservient and maintain British dominance.

(iii.)

  • Increased racial antagonism fueled a 'policy of divide and rule', exploiting existing social divisions.
  • It led to limited opportunities for Indians in administration and the army.
  • This created lasting resentment and contributed to the growth of Indian nationalism.
  • The racial segregation fostered a sense of alienation and inferiority among Indians, with the consequences lasting well into the 20th century.

questions.question_text:
Study the image.

(i.) Identify the person in the image. [3]

(ii.) What disrespectful actions were taken against him by the British? [3]

(iii.) How did this disrespect contribute to the revolt of 1857? [4]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • The person in the image is Bahadur Shah Zafar, the last Mughal Emperor.
  • He was a symbol of the Mughal dynasty.
  • He was seen as a figurehead by many Indians.

(ii.)

  • The British stopped recognizing him as Emperor.
  • Lord Dalhousie announced that Bahadur Shah Zafar's successors would not be recognised as Emperors.
  • Lord Canning announced that after Bahadur Shah's death, his successors would not be allowed to use the imperial titles.

(iii.)

  • The disrespect shown to Bahadur Shah Zafar angered many Indians, especially Muslims, who still held him in high regard.
  • It was seen as an affront to Indian tradition and heritage, contributing to the growing resentment against British rule.
  • This disrespect helped to unite various groups against the British, as it symbolized the erosion of Indian power and authority.
  • The disrespect was a political cause of the revolt of 1857 as it hurt the sentiments of the people, providing a rallying point for the sepoys and other leaders.

questions.question_text:
Study the image.

(i.) Identify the person in the image and briefly describe his position in 1857. [3]

(ii.) Explain briefly the significance of Delhi in the context of the 1857 revolt. [3]

(iii.) Discuss briefly the consequences faced by this individual after the British regained control of Delhi. [4]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • The person in the image is Bahadur Shah Zafar.
  • He was the last Mughal Emperor of India in 1857.
  • He was a figurehead leader, with limited real political power before the revolt.

(ii.)

  • Delhi held immense symbolic importance as the former Mughal capital.
  • Seizure of Delhi by the sepoys gave the revolt legitimacy and a rallying point.
  • It was a crucial strategic location that both sides aimed to control.

(iii.)

  • After the British regained control of Delhi, Bahadur Shah Zafar was arrested.
  • He was tried for treason and found guilty.
  • He was exiled to Rangoon (present-day Myanmar).
  • His sons were executed, marking the end of the Mughal dynasty and a powerful symbolic statement by the British.

questions.question_text:
Study the image.

(i.) Identify the historical event most likely depicted in the image, and name any one key figure associated with it. [3]

(ii.) Explain how the 'Policy of Expansion by Outright Wars' contributed to the circumstances leading up to the 1857 revolt. [3]

(iii.) What was the ultimate significance of these outright wars for British control in India in the long run? [4]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • The image likely depicts a battle from one of the Anglo-Sikh Wars or the Anglo-Mysore Wars.
  • A key figure associated with these wars is Lord Dalhousie or Tipu Sultan, respectively.

(ii.)

  • The 'Policy of Expansion by Outright Wars' was aggressive and caused resentment among Indian rulers.
  • The wars of expansion led to annexation of territories like Sindh and Punjab.
  • These annexations destabilized the existing power structures and created widespread fear and discontent amongst the Indian population.

(iii.)

  • Initially, these wars strengthened British control by expanding their territory and influence.
  • However, the constant wars and annexations fueled resentment and anti-British sentiment amongst the Indian population.
  • This resentment contributed significantly to the outbreak of the 1857 revolt.
  • While the British ultimately suppressed the revolt, it forced them to reassess their policies and adopt a more cautious approach towards expansion and governance in India.

questions.question_text:
Study the image.

(i.) Briefly explain what the Subsidiary Alliance was. [3]

(ii.) What were the main features imposed on Indian rulers by the Subsidiary Alliance? [3]

(iii.) How did the Subsidiary Alliance contribute to the growing resentment against British rule, ultimately leading to the First War of Independence? [4]
questions.model_answer_text:
(i.)

  • The Subsidiary Alliance was a treaty system devised by Lord Wellesley to expand British influence and control in India.
  • It essentially meant that Indian rulers entering the alliance had to accept British forces in their territory and pay for their maintenance.
  • In return, the British promised to protect them from external threats and internal rebellions.

(ii.)

  • Indian rulers had to disband their own armies, rendering them dependent on the British for military protection.
  • They were required to accept a British Resident in their court, who effectively controlled their administration and foreign policy.
  • They could not enter into alliances or negotiations with other powers without British consent.

(iii.)

  • The Subsidiary Alliance led to a loss of independence for Indian rulers, as their autonomy was severely curtailed.
  • The financial burden of maintaining British troops drained their resources and impoverished their kingdoms.
  • The presence of British Residents interfered in their internal affairs, creating resentment and discontent among the people.
  • This erosion of power and authority fueled anti-British sentiment, contributing to the underlying causes of the 1857 revolt.
questions.section_ref: The First War of Independence > CAUSES OF THE FIRST WAR OF INDEPENDENCE > Political Causes > Policy of Expansion > By Subsidiary Alliance

questions.question_text: Ranjit Singh's death led to annexation of Punjab. How did this 'outright war' policy impact Indian sentiment towards the British?
questions.model_answer_text:
• It created resentment among the Sikhs who were proud of their kingdom and felt humiliated by the annexation.
  • It instilled fear among other rulers who saw the British as aggressive and expansionist.
  • It deepened distrust of the British intentions and fueled anti-British feelings among the general population.
questions.section_ref: Political Causes > Policy of Expansion > By Outright Wars

questions.question_text: If Awadh had rejected the Subsidiary Alliance, what two contrasting consequences might have unfolded for the British East India Company?
questions.model_answer_text:
• Increased Military Burden: The British might have faced a constant threat from a potentially hostile and independent Awadh, requiring a larger military presence and expenditure to secure their existing territories. This would strain resources and manpower.
  • Reduced Influence: Without the control afforded by the Subsidiary Alliance, the British would have had less influence over Awadh's internal affairs and foreign policy. This could have limited their ability to exploit the region's resources and strategic location, potentially hindering their overall expansionist agenda in India.
questions.section_ref: Political Causes > Policy of Expansion > By Subsidiary Alliance

questions.question_text: Imagine a kingdom annexed under the Doctrine of Lapse. How might its former nobility react, and what specific grievances might they express? Refer to Political Causes > Policy of Expansion > By Using the Doctrine of Lapse.
questions.model_answer_text:
• Loss of Power and Status: The former nobility would deeply resent the loss of their hereditary power, titles, and administrative positions, now replaced by British officials.
  • Economic Hardship: The annexation often led to the confiscation of jagirs (land grants) and other sources of revenue, causing economic hardship for the nobility.
  • Humiliation and Disrespect: The nobility felt humiliated and disrespected by the British, who often treated them with disdain and ignored their traditional customs and privileges. This insult would be a key grievance.
  • Fear for Future Generations: The loss of inheritance rights and the uncertain future for their families created a sense of fear and resentment towards the British, who were seen as a threat to their lineage and legacy.
questions.section_ref: Political Causes > Policy of Expansion > By Using the Doctrine of Lapse

questions.question_text: If the British annexed Awadh solely due to alleged misrule, what problems might this create in other regions under British control?
questions.model_answer_text:
1. Erosion of Trust: Other rulers would fear similar annexations based on subjective British assessments, leading to distrust and potential unrest.
  2. Instability and Resistance: Knowing their territories were vulnerable, rulers might secretly organize resistance against British rule to prevent annexation.
  3. Increased Scrutiny: British officials would face increased scrutiny regarding their assessment of "misrule," potentially hindering governance and creating bureaucratic delays.
  4. Justification for Rebellion: Annexation based on "misrule" provided a rallying cry for those seeking to overthrow British authority, framing British rule as arbitrary and unjust.
questions.section_ref: Political Causes > Policy of Expansion > On the Pretext of Alleged Misrule
